design_philosophy.core_loop: First-person overworld exploration with sprite-scaled pseudo-3D navigation, transitioning to side-view action-RPG gameplay in towns and dungeons for combat and interaction
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Player-driven undirected exploration balanced against actionable combat encounters; navigating a complex world with minimal guidance
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Traversing a dragon-inhabited fantasy world through immersive first-person perspective, discovering locations organically without forced direction
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unconventional hybrid: combines a custom pseudo-3D overworld engine (sprite scaling without Super FX) with side-scrolling beat-em-up combat. Emphasizes exploration freedom over narrative scaffolding; well-translated first-person narrative delivery creates intimate player perspective rare for SNES RPGs

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player-driven exploration means difficulty scales with preparation; undirected design rewards thorough grinding and careful route planning

progression.unlock_mechanism: Exploration-based; player discovers dungeons, towns, and story progression through free navigation rather than linear gating
replayability_hooks: Multiple exploration routes through unguided overworld
Character progression tied to player discovery pace

world_building_techniques: First-person perspective narrative framing creates intimate storytelling
Environmental exploration reveals world structure (terrain boundaries suggest kingdom divisions)
NPC dialogue in town sequences contextualizes discoveries

faction_indicators: Dragon-inhabited world suggests natural adversarial ecology
Town/dungeon separation implies civilization vs. wilderness divide
danger_signals: Pseudo-3D overworld suggests vast, potentially hostile terrain
Side-scrolling beat-em-up combat indicates frequent dangerous encounters
environmental_storytelling: Mountainlike terrain boundaries enclose distinct regions, suggesting political/geographical separation
Transition from overworld to detailed town/dungeon views implies cultural centers in hostile lands
